coredata.dc:title: Facilepreparationofhighlyfluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive Fe3+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Hao
Ou, Meigui
Sun, Donghao
Yang, Chunlin
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The results of the transmission electron microscope and atomic force microscopy indicated that the average diameter and height of the homogeneously distributed N-GQDs were approximately 5 nm and less than 10 nm, respectively. The results of the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy confirmed the successful incorporation of the N element into GQDs. The aqueous solution of N-GQDs dispersed in water remained stable and homogeneous at room temperature for 3 months. N-GQDs had a higher fluorescence intensity than GQDs and presented low toxicity and the survival rate of cells (3T3-Swiss albino) was more than 85% in the cytotoxicity test. To better understand the luminescence mechanism, the band gaps of GQDs and N-GQDs were calculated using the density functional theory (DFT). The effect of the pH value on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions was investigated. N-GQDs were applied to selectively detect Fe3+ ions, and the results exhibited a wide linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity and the Fe3+ concentration of 0–100 uM with a detection limit of 0.74 uM. This study provided a simple and efficient synthesis method and proved the ultra-high Fe3+ detection selectivity and sensitivity of N-GQDs, offering the experimental and theoretical basis for N-GQDs’ applications in biosensing, bioimaging, environmental monitoring, etc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Fe3+ detection
Bandgap

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell viability measured by MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay
Characterisation and fluorescence properties of N-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Education of Guizhou Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Guizhou provincial education department projects (Grant No. [2017]016) and Guizhou science and technology projects (Grant No. [2022]031).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a),(b) TEM images, (c) AFM image, and (d) height profiles.
(a) XPS spectra of as-prepared N-GQDs, (b) C1s, (c)N1s, and(d) O1s peaks.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum of N-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths, (b) ultraviolet absorption and excitation spectrum of N-GQDs (the inset: the image of N-GQDs under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV-light (right), (c) fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs and N-GQDs at 360 nm excitation wavelength.
(a) (d) Atom model, (b) (e) energy level diagram, and (c) (f) density of states diagram of GQDs and N-GQDs.
(a) Cytotoxicity of N-GQDs at different concentrations after 48 h of 3T3-Swiss albino, (b) fluorescence microscopy of 3T3-Swiss albino labeled with N-GQDs excitation at 488 nm.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs in the presence of different metal ions, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs and N-GQDs in the presence of Fe3+.
Effect of pH = 3–13 on fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs in the presence of different metal ions, (b) histogram of N-GQDs fluorescence response for different metal ions at a wavelength of 450 nm.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs with different Fe3+ concentrations (0–300 uM), (b) calibration curve of the relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration (each point represents the average value of three measurements).
Energy level diagram of (a) N-GQDs, and (b) N-GQDs with Fe3+ ions.
Schematic diagram of N-GQDs as a fluorescence probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions.
Comparison of Fe3+ fluorescence detection ability of different GQDs.

head.ce:title: Facilepreparationofhighlyfluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive Fe3+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Guizhou, Guiyang, Guizhou 550025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Guizhou, Guiyang, Guizhou 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Local Co-Construction Engineering Laboratory for High Performance Metal Structure Material and Manufacture Technology, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Local Co-Construction Engineering Laboratory for High Performance Metal Structure Material and Manufacture Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Local Co-Construction Engineering Laboratory for High Performance Metal Structure Material and Manufacture Technology, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Guiyang University, Guiyang, Guizhou 550003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Guiyang University, Guiyang, Guizhou 550003, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Guizhou, Guiyang, Guizhou 550025, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The results of the transmission electron microscope and atomic force microscopy indicated that the average diameter and height of the homogeneously distributed N-GQDs were approximately 5 nm and less than 10 nm, respectively. The results of the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy confirmed the successful incorporation of the N element into GQDs. The aqueous solution of N-GQDs dispersed in water remained stable and homogeneous at room temperature for 3 months. N-GQDs had a higher fluorescence intensity than GQDs and presented low toxicity and the survival rate of cells (3T3-Swiss albino) was more than 85% in the cytotoxicity test. To better understand the luminescence mechanism, the band gaps of GQDs and N-GQDs were calculated using the density functional theory (DFT). The effect of the pH value on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions was investigated. N-GQDs were applied to selectively detect Fe3+ ions, and the results exhibited a wide linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity and the Fe3+ concentration of 0–100 uM with a detection limit of 0.74 uM. This study provided a simple and efficient synthesis method and proved the ultra-high Fe3+ detection selectivity and sensitivity of N-GQDs, offering the experimental and theoretical basis for N-GQDs’ applications in biosensing, bioimaging, environmental monitoring, etc.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a type of zero bandgap nanomaterial. Its high chemical inertness on the surface limits its applications in bioimaging, optics, and electronics [1–3]. As a result, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gradually been developed to adjust the bandgap of graphene. Unlike traditional organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs have unusual properties, e.g. good photoluminescence (PL) characteristics [4–7], water-solubility, low cytotoxicity [8–10]environmental pollution, and good biocompatibility [11]. The significant photoluminescence of GQDs comes from the quantum size, edge, and defect effect, and the low toxicity and good water solubility are due to not only the quantum size effect but the modification of surface oxygen-containing functional groups [10]. These unique physico-chemical properties enable GQDs to be widely used in catalysis [12], biosensors [13,14], bioimaging [15], and drug delivery [16].

It is well known that the preparation methods of size-adjustable GQDs generally fall into two broad categories: bottom-up and top-down approaches [17–19]. The top-down process includes hydrothermal cutting, solvothermal cutting, microwave-assisted cracking, electrochemical synthesis and chemical oxidation, and improved Hummer's method [20–25], in which the large-size graphene sheets are cut into small GQDs. Whereas, in the bottom-up method, the graphene flakes with conjugated carbon atoms (i.e. GQDs) are directly synthesized, and organic precursor pyrolysis, solution chemistry, and microwave hydrothermal methods [26–30] fall into this category. The molecular structure and size of GQDs can precisely be tuned; consequently, the bandgap and the respective fluorescent and electrical properties of GQDs can be adjusted in the bottom-up process[31,32]. However, the low quantum yield (QY) and single emission peak limit the potential application of GQDs in nanomolecular sensing and cell imaging. Therefore, the doped GQDs (e.g. N-GQDs) with high QY and tunable emission wavelengths are synthesized not only to improve the photoluminescence efficiency, but also to provide active sites on the GQDs, which widens their catalytic, biological, and sensing applications [33,34].

Among a variety of applications of doped GQDs, ion detection (e.g. Fe3+) by using fluorescence quenching is an important and efficient application[35,36]. Iron is an essential trace element in the human body, however, excessive Fe3+ intake causes people to be poisoned, leading to nausea, vomiting, acute abdominal pain, diarrhea, and fever [37,38]. According to the latest drinking water quality guidelines announced by WHO (World Health Organization), a Fe3+ concentration of less than 0.3 mg/L in drinking water is not harmful to the human body. Therefore, it is essential to accurately detect the concentration of trace Fe3+ in environmental, industrial, and medical samples. Currently, the materials used to detect Fe3+ include semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots, GQDs, etc. [39–41]; however, the Fe3+ fluorescence sensing of GQDs still has problems such as unsatisfactory detection sensitivity, which needs to be further improved[42]. Compared with other quantum dots, N-GQDs are more safe, accurate, repeatable, and feasible in sample analysis. There are several fluorescence quenching mechanisms of N-GQDs induced by Fe3+, one of which is due to the interaction between the metal ions of Fe3+ and the functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs, or due to the aggregation of N-GQDs after the addition of Fe3+ [43].

In this paper, N-GQDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method with pollution-free raw materials, low cost, less time, and green reaction conditions. The practicability of N-GQDs for in vitro fluorescence bioimaging and the Fe3+ detection was also demonstrated, indicating that the prepared N-GQDs with high QY, good solubility, and photostability are a type of promising reagent for sensitive detection and biological target imaging.This study provides a new alternative path for further use of GQDs.

Ammonia, citric acid, and NaOH were commercially purchased (99.9%, Aladdin). The solutions of the metal ions (Ca2+, Fe3+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Al3+, NH4 +, Ba2+, Zn2+) were prepared from their respective metal nitrate (Ca(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, NaNO3, Mg(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Zn(NO3)2, NH4NO3), and all reagents were analytically pure and bought from Sinopharm Group.

A certain amount of monohydrate citric acid was mixed with ammonia and put in a high-pressure reactor to react for 2 h, and then was heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the dark brown mixture that was obtained was dissolved in deionized water, and then 10 mol/mL aqueous solution of NaOH was added dropwise until the pH of the solution reached 7. The obtained light-yellow solution was stirred by a magnetic agitator for 30 min and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 mins to remove large particles, followed by activated dialysis for 48 h with a bag of 3000 Da molecular weight. The obtained N-GQDs nanoparticles were dried in a refrigerated drying box for 48 h and the final yellow solid product was ground into powder for use.

The infrared spectra were recorded on a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS5) using the KBr pellet technique within the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. PL quantum yields were recorded on a steady/transient state fluorescence spectrometer (FLS9800) using BaSO4 as a reflectance standard. The morphology and microstructure of N-GQDs were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20,) at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV, and the height profiles were determined by an atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension ICON). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Kalpha) was utilized to determine the surface chemical composition and bonding states. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption properties were characterized on a UV-5800 spectrophotometer. PL excitation and emission spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse) with a 150 W xenon lamp in the wavelength range of 200–400 nm and 400–700 nm, respectively, with a slit width of 5 nm and a resolution of 4 nm.

CASTEP module derived from density functional theory in software materials studio was used to calculate the GQDs bandgap change after N-doping. The crystal wave function was expanded by a plane-wave basis set with the cut-off energy of 240 eV, and 5 × 5 × 1 supercell and periodic boundary conditions were used to simulate the calculation. The unit cell parameters were as follows: a = 12.299995, b = 12.299995, c = 6.8, α = β = 90°, γ = 120°; the GQDs were fully optimized using the generalized gradient approximation in CASTEP. The main calculation parameter GGA-PBE (Generalized gradient approximations, Perdew-Becke-Ernzerhof) was used to describe the correlation function. The energy standard for self-consistent iteration convergence was set to 1.0 × 10−6 Ha, and other parameters were set at default values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability measured by MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse fibroblast cells (3T3-Swiss albino) were prepared into a single cell suspension, and 5 × 103 cells were inoculated on a 96-well cell culture plate at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 12 h.The suspension of nanoparticles prepared by 10% serum medium was added into the cells with concentrations of 0 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, 200 μg/mL, 300 μg/mL, 400 μg/mL, 500 μg/mL, 6 multiple wells in each group.The cells were cultured for 48 h under the same conditions. Then, MTT (5 mg/mL) solution 20 μL was added and the cells continued to be cultured for 4 h.150 µL DI methyl sulfoxide was added to each well by vigorously shaking for 40 min. The absorbance value was measured at 488 nm to calculate the survival rate and analyze the cell viability.

The equivalent Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations (0–300 uM) were added to a 1 mg/mL aqueous solution of N-GQDs at room temperature. Fluorescence was measured at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and the correlation between the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs and the concentration of Fe3+ was calibrated at the concentration range from 0 to 300 uM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation and fluorescence properties of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 presents the TEM and AFM images of the prepared N-GQDs. As seen in the figures, the N-GQDs are elliptical particles with a size ranging from 4 to 6 nm and present good dispersibility in Fig. 1(a). The interplanar spacing can be measured to be approximately 0.24 nm from the lattice fringes of N-GQDs displayed in the inset of the High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image Fig. 1(b), which corresponds to the (1120) crystal plane of graphene [40]. AFM image (Fig. 1(c)) also presents the homogeneously distributed N-GQDs with a size of less than 10 nm (consistent with the TEM images) and a statistical height of N-GQDs presented in Fig. 1(d) is less than 1.5 nm, which proves that the N-GQDs contain 5 layers of graphene (the thickness of single-layer graphene is approximately 0.35 nm).

FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 (a)) presents the surface functional groups of N-GQDs. The peaks at 1656 cm−1 and 1573 cm−1 are attributed to the C=O and N-H tensile vibration. A relatively small peak at 2948 cm-1 displays the presence of N-H bonds in N-GQDs. The obvious absorption peak at 1405 cm−1 is caused by bond bending vibration, which indicates that the N atoms were successfully introduced into the GQDs. The surface passivation peak at 3433 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic peak of O-H. The large number of amino and carboxyl functional groups (the peaks at 1573 cm−1 and 3433 cm−1 ) on the surface of N-GQDs enable N-GQDs to have good dispersion and high hydrophilicity, PBS solution, and other water-soluble substances [41,44]. The Raman spectra of N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2(b), and the two peaks at 1342 cm−1 and 1566 cm−1 are the D and G peaks of graphene, respectively; the D peak is amorphous carbon and the G peak is ordered graphitic carbon, which indicates the formation of N-GQDs. ID/IG is the ratio of the peak intensity of the D peak to that of the G peak, and a larger ID/IG indicates a greater degree of edge defects; this material, with an ID/IG value of 0.93, indicates a high number of defects and a high degree of graphitization, which is favorable to increase the ion and electron migration rates and can improve its fluorescence intensity to some extent [45].

The chemical compositions and surface groups of N-GQDs were studied by XPS as presented in Fig. 3 . Three prominent peaks located at 288.1 eV, 400 eV, and 534 eV attribute to C1s, N1s, and O1s [46], and their atomic concentrations are 64.07%, 4.8%, and 31.12%, respectively. The peak of Na is also observed in Fig. 3 (a), owing to the added NaOH during the synthesis process. The C1s peak illustrated in Fig. 3(b) can be divided into four peaks centered at 284.2 eV, 284.5 eV, 287.8 eV, and 288 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O, C=O/C=N, and-COOH. The presence of C-C/C=C and C-N/C-O indicates that C atoms in graphite interact with O and N atoms. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s in Fig. 3(c) indicates the presence of N(C-N-C) (399.4 eV) and N or N-H bands in the graphite (400 eV). The O1s peaks can be fitted at 531, 532.2, and 535.2 eV, representing C=O and C-OH/C-O-C groups (Fig. 4 (d)). The above XPS results indicate that N atoms are triumphantly doped in the GQDs and that N-GQDs have a lot of functional groups containing N, O, and C atoms, and are coincident with the FTIR results (Fig. 2). The surface of N-GQDs functionalized by functional groups containing O and N elements makes them have good hydrophilicity and the ability to be further modified [47,48].

Fig. 4(a) presents the fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–390 nm), and the maximal excitation and emission wavelengths of N-GQDs are located at 360 nm and 460 nm, respectively. The fluorescence peaks of N-GQDs don’t undergo a significant red or blue shift with the excitation wavelength changing from 300 nm to 390 nm, which indicates that N-GQDs emit blue fluorescence that is independent of the excitation wavelength. N-GQDs in nanometer size have a pronounced quantum confinement effect. The electrons inside the N-GQDs undergo energy level transitions under light irradiation, and the extra energy is radiated in the form of blue fluorescence. The relatively uniform particle size weakens the self-absorption of N-GQDs. It can be observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4 (b)), with a shoulder peak at 230 nm indicating the p-p* transition of the aromatic structure, and a broad peak at 320 nm representing the n-p* transition of the multifunctional groups and sp2 structure [49,50]. The inset in Fig. 4 (b) confirms that the N-GQDs emit bright blue fluorescence under 360 nm ultraviolet light. Fig. 4 (c) illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs is much higher than that of the GQDs, because of the generation of N-doped defects during the preparation of GQDs. After the N atoms occupy the positions of the original C atoms in the GQDs and break away, the nitrogen vacancies will be left in the positions and emit fluorescence excited by visible light [51,52].

To further clarify the reason for the increase in the intensity of fluorescence of N-GQDs, the QY of GQDs and N-GQDs were calculated based on the following equation:
Φ =
Φ R
×
I /
I R
×
A R
/ A
×
η 2
/
η R 2

Where Φ is the QY, A is the optical density, I is the integrated PL intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and the subscript R refers to the quinine sulfate used as a comparison (Φ = 0.54 at 360 nm). Under the excitation of λex = 360 nm, the QY of GQDs and N-GQDs is approximately 1.7% and 19.10%, respectively, indicating that N-doping significantly improves the QY of GQDs. Therefore, the increase in fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs compared with GQDs is also probably because of the increase in QY. The increase in QY may be attributed to the surface state of introduced nitrogen doping, which is beneficial for electron trapping to facilitate a high yield of radiative recombination.

The band gaps of GQDs and N-GQDs were calculated by the DFT to demonstrate the change in the electronic structure and fluorescent property induced by the N-doping, and the calculation results are presented in Fig. 5 . The atom models used for the calculation are displayed in Fig. 5 (a) and (d), in which the blue N atom (Fig. 5 (d)) replaces the position of the grey C atom after the N-doping. It can be seen from the energy level diagram in Fig. 5 (b) and 5 (e) that the bandgap of N-doped GQDs (0.249 eV) is smaller than that of the undoped GQDs (2.826 eV). A smaller bandgap means a more energy-efficient electron transition to higher energy levels and more activation sites can be formed [53]. To clarify the effect of heteroatom doping on quantum dots, the electronic structure can be analyzed by calculating the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS). Fig. 5 (c) and 5 (f) present the density of states diagram of GQDs and N-GQDs.As illustrated in Fig. 5 (c), TDOS and PDOS of GQDs are composed of S orbitals and P orbitals, and the N-doping does not change their orbital composition. However, compared with the TDOS of GQDs, the intensity change of TDOS for N-GQDs is obvious, and the intensity of PDOS of GQDs is stronger than that of N-GQDs. This change of N-GQDs indicates that the electronic transition mainly occurs on N atoms. In summary, the active sites provided by the smaller bandgap after the doping of N atoms and the electronic transitions occurring on the N atoms can enhance the fluorescence properties of GQDs.

The ideal fluorescent probe should have characteristics such as high fluorescence, biocompatibility, and non-toxicity to biological systems. The cytotoxicity of N-GQDs with different concentrations was measured after being incubated with a mouse fibroblast cell for 48 h (Fig. 6 (a)). The cell viability remains greater than 85% with the concentration of N-GQDs increasing from 100 to 500 ug/mL. Even if the concentration of N-GQDs reaches 500 μg/mL, the cell viability doesn’t significantly decrease. Fig. 6 (b) presents the fluorescence microscopy of the sample under the excitation at 488 nm, respectively. 3T3-Swiss albino cells incubated with N-GQDs exhibit bright green fluorescence under 488 nm excitation, which is mainly concentrated in the cytoplasm and indicates that the N-GQDs with small sizes enter the cytoplasm through gene-free endocytosis. Apart from the unique optical properties, the N-GQDs also have good stability. After 3 months of storage at room temperature, the N-GQDs still have high PL intensity. Therefore, the N-GQDs have broad application prospects in biological imaging and light-sensing [54].

In order to explore the application of N-GQDs in metal ions’ detection, the influence of different metal ions (Fe3+,Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Al3+, NH4+, Ba2+, Zn2+) on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs are firstly investigated. As presented in Fig. 7 (a),the fluorescence intensity of GQDs significantly decreases in the presence of Fe3+. However, there is no decrease in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of other metal ions with the same concentration, except for Cu2+ and Ni+, which induces a little quenching effect on GQDs. The comparison of fluorescence intensity between GQDs and N-GQDs in the presence of Fe3+ is illustrated in Fig. 7 (b). It can be seen that the decrease in fluorescence intensity is more obvious for N-GQDs than GQDs, which indicates that N-GQDs have better Fe3+ sensitivity than GQDs, because Fe3+ may have stronger interactions with the “N” and “O” on the surface of N-GQDs [55].

Subsequently, to determine the optimal pH environment for Fe3+ ions detection, the effect of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions were investigated. Fig. 8 presents the luminescence intensity of N-GQDs at pH values within the range 3–13.In the absence of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity increases with an increase in the pH value in acidic conditions and then decreases in alkaline conditions, and the fluorescence intensity reaches its maximum value at pH = 7. After the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence quenching occurs and the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs decreases significantly at all pH values. The fluorescence quenching efficiency increases gradually under acidic conditions (pH < 7), and decreases gradually under alkaline conditions (pH > 7) and achieves the maximum value at pH = 7.In a strong acidic solution, due to the protonation of the carboxyl group on the N-GQDs surface, the electron sea density of N-GQDs decreases, and the binding force between the N-GQDs and Fe3+ decreases, resulting in a relatively low fluorescence quenching efficiency [56]. With the increase of pH value, the carboxyl group on the surface of N-GQDs is gradually deprotonated, and the binding force between the N-GQDs and Fe3+ gradually increases, leading to the increase in fluorescence quenching efficiency.Especially at pH = 13, the fluorescence quenching efficiency is the lowest because in alkaline conditions, Fe3+ prefers to bind to the basic group, and weakens the binding with other functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs [57].Therefore, pH = 7 is selected to perform the following Fe3+ detecting test.

In order to further illustrate the effective selectivity of N-GQDs to Fe3+ ions detection, the influence of different metal ions (Ca2+, Na+, Fe3+,Mg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Al3+, NH4+, Ba2+, Zn2+) on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs were investigated. The suspension of N-GQDs was adjusted to a neutral condition (pH = 7) beforehand, so that metal ions can exist in a free form. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs significantly decreases, whereas no inconspicuous fluorescence emission quenching was observed when other metals with the same concentration were added to the N-GQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 9 (a), the fluorescence of N-GQDs shows a specific response to Fe3+, which indicates that N-GQDs can be used as a selective fluorescence sensor for Fe3+. The fluorescence extinction may be related to the particular affinity between Fe3+ and the oxygen/nitrogen functional groups such as -NH2, -COOH, or -OH on the surface of N-GQDs [58–60]. Owing to the paramagnetic property of Fe3+, the electron-deficient Fe3+ on the surface of N-GQDs has a high binding affinity with the electron-rich -OH or -NH2 groups [61]. The unfilled electron orbit of Fe3+ is favorable for the transfer of excited state electrons coming from N-GQDs [62]. The recombination of the resulting non-radiative electrons and the holes directly lead to the fluorescence quenching of the N-GQDs.

To clarify the PL quenching mechanism of N-GQDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions, the energy bands were calculated based on the DFT, and the energy level diagram is in Fig. 11 . The band gap of N-GQDs with Fe3+ increases from 0.249 to 1.072 eV, and the relative wide bandgap (1.072 eV) reduces the number of activation sites which is not conducive to the electron transition to a high energy level, and thus inhibits the luminescence.The PL quenching mechanism is related to the affinity and fast chelation kinetics.The radiative recombination of excited electron-hole pairs in N-GQDs is strongly suppressed by chelation through a rapid electron transfer process from N-GQDs to Fe3+ ions, where N-GQDs acts as an electron donor and binds to Fe3+ to form a non-fluorescent complex [67,68] in a process shown in Fig. 12 .

Fluorescent N-GQDs with an average diameter of 5 nm were prepared by the facile hydrothermal method. The prepared N-GQDs had good optical properties and their intensity of fluorescence was higher than that of the GQDs.Its quantum yield of 19.1% is much higher than that of GQDs (1.7%). The band gaps of N-GQDs were smaller than that of the undoped GQDs and provides more active sites, which makes the N-GQDs more prone to electronic transition and thus helps to improve the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs.In the measurement of cytotoxicity of the cell activity remained greater than 85%.The prepared N-GQDs can be used for the specific and sensitive detection of Fe3+ with a linear range of 0–100 uM and a detection limit of 0.74 uM, which is lower than the highest permissible value of Fe3+ in drinking water allowed by WHO.The results indicate that the N-GQDs synthesized in this study have important value in designing effective fluorescent probes and detecting metal ions in the environment.

Hao Zhou: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Meigui Ou: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Donghao Sun: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Chunlin Yang: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Guizhou provincial education department projects (Grant No. [2017]016) and Guizhou science and technology projects (Grant No. [2022]031).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: L.S. Li, X. Yan, Colloidal graphene quantum dots, J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 1.17 (2010) 2572–2576. https://doi.org/10.1021/jz100862f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Covalent functionalization of graphene with reactive intermediates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.G. Li, Z. Li,S. Fan, Z. Gu, J. Liu, D-penicillamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots for fluorescent detection of Fe3+ in iron supplement oral liquids, Sensor. Actuator. B-Chem. 243(2017)211-220. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.11.150.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical synthesis of multicolor fluorescent N-doped graphene quantum dots as a ferric ion sensor and their application in bioimagin
J. Mater. Chem. B

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of nitrogen-doping carbon dots with different photoluminescence properties by controlling the surface states
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yuan YH, Liu ZX, Li RS, Zou HY, Lin M, Liu H, Huang CZ, Synthesis of nitrogen-doping carbon dots with different photoluminescence properties by controlling the surface states. Nanoscale 8(2016)6770–6776. https://doi.org/10.1039/C6NR00402D.

Nanomaterial

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, T. Zou, Z. Wang, X. Xing, S. Peng, R. Zhao, X. Zhang, Y. Wang, The fluorescent quenching mechanism of N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots with Fe3+ and Hg2+ Ions and their application as an ovel fluorescent sensor, Nanomaterial 9 (2019)738. https://doi.org/10.3390/nano9050738.
